text: [music playing]

Each and every one of us
has two ears coated in wax.
That's weird.

But have you ever
stopped and wondered

what earwax actually is,
and, more importantly,
why do we have it?

Let's find out on today's
episode of Colossal Questions.

The stuff we call earwax has a
more scientific name, cerumen.

It's made by glands
deep in the ear canal

and slowly oozes its way
towards the opening of the ear.

As icky as it might seem,
earwax is actually good for you.

The sticky cerumen acts
like a protective barrier

for the super sensitive
skin inside your ear.

The earwax does a lot to
protect the delicate parts
of your inner ear.

It traps dirt and germs,
it keeps water out,

and it collects bacteria
and other dusty allergens
and irritants.

Usually, earwax naturally
disappears over time,

eventually moving
from deep in the ear

all the way out to the opening,
where it falls, flakes,
or washes away.

Then, fresh, new
earwax is created,
and the cycle starts again.

Sometimes wax can
build up over time

if your glands are producing
too much of the stuff.

When that happens, the
extra wax can dry up

and harden, causing
a blockage that can
hurt or make it hard to hear.

Even though they seem like
the perfect tool for the job,

you really shouldn't
use cotton swabs

to try and get rid
of excess earwax.

Usually, it just pushes the
extra wax deeper and packs it
down, making the problem worse.

The inside of the ear
is very sensitive.

So if you're walking around
with way too much earwax,

you're better off
going to the doctor

rather than trying to
scoop it out yourself.
So why do we have earwax?

To help keep our ears
healthy, even if sometimes it
can get a little bit gross.
[music playing]
